harkness_discussion_prompts.title: Pride and Prejudice — Netherfield and First Impressions
harkness_discussion_prompts.prompt:
You are to write a realistic Harkness discussion DIALOGUE SCRIPT for EN421: The Novel.

COURSE CONTEXT
- Code: EN421
- Title: The Novel
- Description: Devoted to the study of long-form fiction. Reading materials include novels by diverse authors from the nineteenth and twentieth centuries. Analytical writing plays a central role with students composing papers on each of three novels. Discussion-based inquiry around the Harkness table.
- Tags: novel, long-form fiction, analytical writing, Harkness, 19th century, 20th century

SCHOOL CONTEXT
- Lawrenceville School, boarding school, grades 9-12
- Students are strong readers and capable of discussion at an undergraduate seminar level
- The classroom uses the Harkness method: a student-led discussion around a table

THE HARKNESS DISCUSSION METHOD
Harkness discussion is a student-led, collaborative inquiry method developed at Phillips Exeter Academy. It is the cornerstone pedagogy at the Lawrenceville School and other leading independent schools.

SETUP: Students sit in an oval or rectangle (the "Harkness table") so every participant can see every other participant. The teacher sits at the table as an equal presence but does NOT moderate or call on students.

STUDENT ROLE: Students drive the conversation. They are expected to:
- Reference the text directly (open books on the table, cite passages)
- Build on, challenge, and extend each other's ideas
- Ask follow-up questions of each other (not the teacher)
- Tolerate productive silence — not every pause needs filling
- Disagree respectfully and with textual evidence

TEACHER ROLE: The teacher is an observer and occasional catalyst. The teacher:
- Opens with a single, rich question and then steps back
- Maps the discussion (tracks who speaks, who builds on whom)
- Intervenes ONLY to redirect a stalled discussion, push deeper, or surface a neglected thread — never to provide "the answer"
- Closes the discussion with a synthesis move that helps students name what they collectively discovered

WHAT MAKES A GOOD HARKNESS QUESTION:
- It is TEXT-ANCHORED: students must have the text open to answer it
- It is GENUINELY CONTESTED: reasonable readers can disagree
- It is GENERATIVE: it opens up multiple lines of inquiry, not one right path
- It is ACCESSIBLE: every student can enter the conversation, but no student can exhaust it quickly
- It avoids yes/no framing and avoids questions with obvious "right" answers

DISCUSSION ARC: A good Harkness session typically moves through:
1. ENTRY — students orient to the question, offer initial readings
2. EXPLORATION — competing interpretations surface, evidence is marshalled
3. COMPLICATION — students discover tensions, contradictions, or layers
4. SYNTHESIS — the group begins to articulate a collective (not unanimous) insight

TEXT FOR THIS DISCUSSION
Novel: Pride and Prejudice by Jane Austen
Focus: Chapters 27–32, especially the Netherfield ball, Darcy and Elizabeth’s exchanges, and the aftermath of Mr. Collins’s proposal to Charlotte Lucas.

Use these verbatim passages in the students’ dialogue. Students should cite chapter numbers and quote exactly where possible.

1) Elizabeth to Darcy at the Netherfield ball, Chapter 18:
“Is your family very large, Mr. Darcy?”
“No; there are not many of us.”
“Then you are not likely to miss a train of listeners, for we are all talkers and not listeners.”

2) Darcy’s first proposal in Chapter 34 is not the main focus, but students may allude to the social logic that builds toward it. Use this shorter line if needed:
“She is tolerable; but not handsome enough to tempt me; and I am in no humour at present to give consequence to young ladies who are slighted by other men.”

3) Elizabeth reflecting on Darcy after the ball, Chapter 18:
“I could easily forgive his pride, if he had not mortified mine.”

4) On Mr. Collins and Charlotte, Chapter 22:
“Mr. Collins was not a sensible man, and the deficiency of nature had been but little assisted by education or society.”

5) Charlotte’s rationale, Chapter 22:
“I am not romantic, you know. I never was. I ask only a comfortable home; and considering Mr. Collins’s character, connections, and situation in life, I am convinced that my chance of happiness with him is as fair as most people can boast on entering the marriage state.”

6) Elizabeth and Jane on character and first impressions, Chapter 6:
“But people themselves alter so much, that there is something new to be observed in them for ever.”

7) Darcy on his own reserve, Chapter 34, useful as foreshadowing:
“I have not the talent which some people possess, of conversing easily with those I have never seen before.”

8) A key Austen line for social judgment, Chapter 5:
“A lady’s imagination is very rapid; it jumps from admiration to love, from love to matrimony, in a moment.”

YOUR TASK
Write an ACTUAL dialogue script, not an essay, not notes, not a summary.

The script must feel like a realistic 20-30 minute Harkness table discussion written as a transcript/play script with:
- 6-8 named students using first names only, e.g. Maya, James, Sofia, Ethan, Aaliyah, Noah, Priya
- Distinct voices and perspectives
- The TEACHER who opens with one rich question, then steps back and only intervenes 2-3 times to redirect or deepen
- Students who quote and reference the text directly in their dialogue, citing chapter numbers and/or page references if convenient
- At least one genuine interpretive disagreement
- Moments of productive silence or uncertainty
- A student changing their mind or refining their position
- Natural arc: entry/orientation, exploration of competing readings, complication/tension, and movement toward synthesis
- Stage directions in brackets for key moments: [pause], [flipping pages], [nodding], [turning to address another student], etc.
- Real high school voices: smart, engaged, articulate, but still teenagers — not professors

OPENING QUESTION FOR THE TEACHER
The teacher should begin with exactly one rich question about how Austen uses social performance, first impressions, and self-knowledge in these chapters to make us rethink who is actually being read accurately by whom.

STYLE AND CONSTRAINTS
- The dialogue should be text-anchored throughout
- Do not turn this into a literary analysis essay
- Do not include headings, bullet points, or explanatory commentary
- Do not have the teacher lecture
- Make the discussion feel alive, messy, and authentic
- End with the teacher’s brief synthesis move that names one or two insights the group has discovered without fully resolving them

Output only the script itself.

harkness_discussion_prompts.title: Great Expectations — Satis House and Social Aspiration
harkness_discussion_prompts.prompt:
You are to write a realistic Harkness discussion DIALOGUE SCRIPT for EN421: The Novel.

COURSE CONTEXT
- Code: EN421
- Title: The Novel
- Description: Devoted to the study of long-form fiction. Reading materials include novels by diverse authors from the nineteenth and twentieth centuries. Analytical writing plays a central role with students composing papers on each of three novels. Discussion-based inquiry around the Harkness table.
- Tags: novel, long-form fiction, analytical writing, Harkness, 19th century, 20th century

SCHOOL CONTEXT
- Lawrenceville School, boarding school, grades 9-12
- Students are strong readers and capable of discussion at an undergraduate seminar level
- The classroom uses the Harkness method: a student-led discussion around a table

THE HARKNESS DISCUSSION METHOD
Harkness discussion is a student-led, collaborative inquiry method developed at Phillips Exeter Academy. It is the cornerstone pedagogy at the Lawrenceville School and other leading independent schools.

SETUP: Students sit in an oval or rectangle (the "Harkness table") so every participant can see every other participant. The teacher sits at the table as an equal presence but does NOT moderate or call on students.

STUDENT ROLE: Students drive the conversation. They are expected to:
- Reference the text directly (open books on the table, cite passages)
- Build on, challenge, and extend each other's ideas
- Ask follow-up questions of each other (not the teacher)
- Tolerate productive silence — not every pause needs filling
- Disagree respectfully and with textual evidence

TEACHER ROLE: The teacher is an observer and occasional catalyst. The teacher:
- Opens with a single, rich question and then steps back
- Maps the discussion (tracks who speaks, who builds on whom)
- Intervenes ONLY to redirect a stalled discussion, push deeper, or surface a neglected thread — never to provide "the answer"
- Closes the discussion with a synthesis move that helps students name what they collectively discovered

WHAT MAKES A GOOD HARKNESS QUESTION:

- It is ACCESSIBLE: every student can enter the conversation, but no student can exhaust it quickly
- It avoids yes/no framing and avoids questions with obvious "right" answers

DISCUSSION ARC: A good Harkness session typically moves through:
1. ENTRY — students orient to the question, offer initial readings
2. EXPLORATION — competing interpretations surface, evidence is marshalled
3. COMPLICATION — students discover tensions, contradictions, or layers
4. SYNTHESIS — the group begins to articulate a collective (not unanimous) insight

TEXT FOR THIS DISCUSSION
Novel: Great Expectations by Charles Dickens
Focus: Chapters 8–9, especially Pip’s first visit to Satis House, his meeting with Estella, and Miss Havisham’s influence on his self-image.

Use these verbatim passages in the students’ dialogue. Students should cite chapter numbers and quote exactly where possible.

1) Pip on the atmosphere of Satis House, Chapter 8:
“[The house] was of old brick, and dismal, and had a gloomy appearance.”

2) Pip on Miss Havisham, Chapter 8:
“Once, I had the dreadful impression that she was a skeleton in the chair, and that she had ceased to live many years ago.”

3) Miss Havisham’s instruction to Estella, Chapter 8:
“Break their hearts my pride and hope, break their hearts and have no mercy!”

4) Estella to Pip, Chapter 8:
“He calls the knaves, Jacks, this boy! And what coarse hands he has! And what thick boots!”

5) Pip’s humiliation, Chapter 8:
“I wished Joe had been rather more genteelly brought up, and then I should have been so too.”

6) Pip on his shame, Chapter 9:
“I took the opportunity of being more snug and solitary than usual.”

7) A crucial line for moral ambivalence, Chapter 9:
“Of course, there was no reason why I should not have been ashamed of the village; but I was ashamed of the village, and of Joe, and of myself.”

8) Dickens’s self-reflexive social critique, Chapter 29, for foreshadowing if students want it:
“Never say die!”

YOUR TASK
Write an ACTUAL dialogue script, not an essay, not notes, not a summary.

The script must feel like a realistic 20-30 minute Harkness table discussion written as a transcript/play script with:
- 6-8 named students using first names only, e.g. Maya, James, Sofia, Ethan, Aaliyah, Noah, Priya
- Distinct voices and perspectives
- The TEACHER who opens with one rich question, then steps back and only intervenes 2-3 times to redirect or deepen
- Students who quote and reference the text directly in their dialogue, citing chapter numbers and/or page references if convenient
- At least one genuine interpretive disagreement
- Moments of productive silence or uncertainty
- A student changing their mind or refining their position
- Natural arc: entry/orientation, exploration of competing readings, complication/tension, and movement toward synthesis
- Stage directions in brackets for key moments: [pause], [flipping pages], [nodding], [turning to address another student], etc.
- Real high school voices: smart, engaged, articulate, but still teenagers — not professors

OPENING QUESTION FOR THE TEACHER
The teacher should begin with exactly one rich question about whether Dickens presents Satis House as merely a place of corruption and cruelty, or as something more psychologically complex that reveals how shame gets taught to Pip.

STYLE AND CONSTRAINTS
- The dialogue should be text-anchored throughout
- Do not turn this into a literary analysis essay
- Do not include headings, bullet points, or explanatory commentary
- Do not have the teacher lecture
- Make the discussion feel alive, messy, and authentic
- End with the teacher’s brief synthesis move that names one or two insights the group has discovered without fully resolving them

Output only the script itself.

harkness_discussion_prompts.title: Beloved — Memory, Ghosts, and Motherhood
harkness_discussion_prompts.prompt:
You are to write a realistic Harkness discussion DIALOGUE SCRIPT for EN421: The Novel.

COURSE CONTEXT
- Code: EN421
- Title: The Novel
- Description: Devoted to the study of long-form fiction. Reading materials include novels by diverse authors from the nineteenth and twentieth centuries. Analytical writing plays a central role with students composing papers on each of three novels. Discussion-based inquiry around the Harkness table.
- Tags: novel, long-form fiction, analytical writing, Harkness, 19th century, 20th century

SCHOOL CONTEXT
- Lawrenceville School, boarding school, grades 9-12
- Students are strong readers and capable of discussion at an undergraduate seminar level
- The classroom uses the Harkness method: a student-led discussion around a table

THE HARKNESS DISCUSSION METHOD
Harkness discussion is a student-led, collaborative inquiry method developed at Phillips Exeter Academy. It is the cornerstone pedagogy at the Lawrenceville School and other leading independent schools.

SETUP: Students sit in an oval or rectangle (the "Harkness table") so every participant can see every other participant. The teacher sits at the table as an equal presence but does NOT moderate or call on students.

STUDENT ROLE: Students drive the conversation. They are expected to:
- Reference the text directly (open books on the table, cite passages)
- Build on, challenge, and extend each other's ideas
- Ask follow-up questions of each other (not the teacher)
- Tolerate productive silence — not every pause needs filling
- Disagree respectfully and with textual evidence

TEACHER ROLE: The teacher is an observer and occasional catalyst. The teacher:
- Opens with a single, rich question and then steps back
- Maps the discussion (tracks who speaks, who builds on whom)
- Intervenes ONLY to redirect a stalled discussion, push deeper, or surface a neglected thread — never to provide "the answer"
- Closes the discussion with a synthesis move that helps students name what they collectively discovered

- It is TEXT-ANCHORED: students must have the text open to answer it
- It is GENUINELY CONTESTED: reasonable readers can disagree
- It is GENERATIVE: it opens up multiple lines of inquiry, not one right path
- It is ACCESSIBLE: every student can enter the conversation, but no student can exhaust it quickly
- It avoids yes/no framing and avoids questions with obvious "right" answers

DISCUSSION ARC: A good Harkness session typically moves through:
1. ENTRY — students orient to the question, offer initial readings
2. EXPLORATION — competing interpretations surface, evidence is marshalled
3. COMPLICATION — students discover tensions, contradictions, or layers
4. SYNTHESIS — the group begins to articulate a collective (not unanimous) insight

TEXT FOR THIS DISCUSSION
Novel: Beloved by Toni Morrison
Focus: Chapters 1–3 and 8, especially the haunting at 124, Sethe’s memory of Sweet Home, and the relationship between motherhood and ownership.

Use these verbatim passages in the students’ dialogue. Students should cite chapter numbers and quote exactly where possible.

1) Opening line, Chapter 1:
“124 was spiteful. Full of a baby’s venom.”

2) Sethe’s understanding of the house, Chapter 1:
“It was not a story to pass on.”

3) On the haunting, Chapter 1:
“Dressed in a gown of her own making, made out of an undergarment cut by hand, the baby ghost was everywhere.”

4) Sethe on memory, Chapter 2:
“Freeing yourself was one thing; claiming ownership of that freed self was another.”

5) Paul D on the past, Chapter 2:
“You got two feet, Sethe, not four.”

6) Denver’s sense of isolation, Chapter 3:
“Beloved was my sister.”

7) Baby Suggs on self-love, Chapter 3:
“Love your hands! Love them. Raise them up and kiss them.”

8) A phrase for the pressure of the past, Chapter 8:
“Nothing could be counted on in a world where a baby ghost had learned to talk.”

9) Sethe’s central trauma, Chapter 6, useful if the discussion needs it:
“Nan told her, to take and put one’s babies where they’d be safe.”

YOUR TASK
Write an ACTUAL dialogue script, not an essay, not notes, or a summary.

The script must feel like a realistic 20-30 minute Harkness table discussion written as a transcript/play script with:
- 6-8 named students using first names only, e.g. Maya, James, Sofia, Ethan, Aaliyah, Noah, Priya
- Distinct voices and perspectives
- The TEACHER who opens with one rich question, then steps back and only intervenes 2-3 times to redirect or deepen
- Students who quote and reference the text directly in their dialogue, citing chapter numbers and/or page references if convenient
- At least one genuine interpretive disagreement
- Moments of productive silence or uncertainty
- A student changing their mind or refining their position
- Natural arc: entry/orientation, exploration of competing readings, complication/tension, and movement toward synthesis
- Stage directions in brackets for key moments: [pause], [flipping pages], [nodding], [turning to address another student], etc.
- Real high school voices: smart, engaged, articulate, but still teenagers — not professors

OPENING QUESTION FOR THE TEACHER
The teacher should begin with exactly one rich question about whether the novel treats haunting as supernatural fact, psychological aftermath, communal memory, or some combination of these, and how that shapes our understanding of Sethe as mother.

STYLE AND CONSTRAINTS
- The dialogue should be text-anchored throughout
- Do not turn this into a literary analysis essay
- Do not include headings, bullet points, or explanatory commentary
- Do not have the teacher lecture
- Make the discussion feel alive, messy, and authentic
- End with the teacher’s brief synthesis move that names one or two insights the group has discovered without fully resolving them

Output only the script itself.

harkness_discussion_prompts.title: The Great Gatsby — The Green Light and the American Dream
harkness_discussion_prompts.prompt:
You are to write a realistic Harkness discussion DIALOGUE SCRIPT for EN421: The Novel.

COURSE CONTEXT
- Code: EN421
- Title: The Novel
- Description: Devoted to the study of long-form fiction. Reading materials include novels by diverse authors from the nineteenth and twentieth centuries. Analytical writing plays a central role with students composing papers on each of three novels. Discussion-based inquiry around the Harkness table.
- Tags: novel, long-form fiction, analytical writing, Harkness, 19th century, 20th century

SCHOOL CONTEXT
- Lawrenceville School, boarding school, grades 9-12
- Students are strong readers and capable of discussion at an undergraduate seminar level
- The classroom uses the Harkness method: a student-led discussion around a table

THE HARKNESS DISCUSSION METHOD
Harkness discussion is a student-led, collaborative inquiry method developed at Phillips Exeter Academy. It is the cornerstone pedagogy at the Lawrenceville School and other leading independent schools.

SETUP: Students sit in an oval or rectangle (the "Harkness table") so every participant can see every other participant. The teacher sits at the table as an equal presence but does NOT moderate or call on students.

STUDENT ROLE: Students drive the conversation. They are expected to:
- Reference the text directly (open books on the table, cite passages)
- Build on, challenge, and extend each other's ideas
- Ask follow-up questions of each other (not the teacher)
- Tolerate productive silence — not every pause needs filling
- Disagree respectfully and with textual evidence

TEACHER ROLE: The teacher is an observer and occasional catalyst. The teacher:
- Opens with a single, rich question and then steps back
- Maps the discussion (tracks who speaks, who builds on whom)
- Intervenes ONLY to redirect a stalled discussion, push deeper, or surface a neglected thread — never to provide "the answer"
- Closes the discussion with a synthesis move that helps students name what they collectively discovered

- It is TEXT-ANCHORED: students must have the text open to answer it
- It is GENUINELY CONTESTED: reasonable readers can disagree
- It is GENERATIVE: it opens up multiple lines of inquiry, not one right path
- It is ACCESSIBLE: every student can enter the conversation, but no student can exhaust it quickly
- It avoids yes/no framing and avoids questions with obvious "right" answers

DISCUSSION ARC: A good Harkness session typically moves through:
1. ENTRY — students orient to the question, offer initial readings
2. EXPLORATION — competing interpretations surface, evidence is marshalled
3. COMPLICATION — students discover tensions, contradictions, or layers
4. SYNTHESIS — the group begins to articulate a collective (not unanimous) insight

TEXT FOR THIS DISCUSSION
Novel: The Great Gatsby by F. Scott Fitzgerald
Focus: Chapters 1, 3, and 5, especially Nick’s introduction to Gatsby, the green light, and Gatsby and Daisy’s reunion.

Use these verbatim passages in the students’ dialogue. Students should cite chapter numbers and quote exactly where possible.

1) Nick on Gatsby, Chapter 1:
“If personality is an unbroken series of successful gestures, then there was something gorgeous about him.”

2) The green light, Chapter 1:
“He stretched out his arms toward the dark water in a curious way, and, far as I was from him, I could have sworn he was trembling.”

3) Nick on the American dream, Chapter 1:
“So we beat on, boats against the current, borne back ceaselessly into the past.”

4) Gatsby’s insistence on the past, Chapter 6:
“Can’t repeat the past? Why of course you can!”

5) Daisy’s voice, Chapter 1:
“Her voice is full of money,” he said suddenly.

6) Gatsby and Daisy’s reunion, Chapter 5:
“There must have been moments even that afternoon when Daisy tumbled short of his dreams — not through her own fault, but because of the colossal vitality of his illusion.”

7) Nick on Gatsby’s performance, Chapter 3:
“I am one of the few honest people that I have ever known.”

8) A line for social spectacle, Chapter 3:
“People were not invited — they went there.”

YOUR TASK
Write an ACTUAL dialogue script, not an essay, not notes, or a summary.

The script must feel like a realistic 20-30 minute Harkness table discussion written as a transcript/play script with:
- 6-8 named students using first names only, e.g. Maya, James, Sofia, Ethan, Aaliyah, Noah, Priya
- Distinct voices and perspectives
- The TEACHER who opens with one rich question, then steps back and only intervenes 2-3 times to redirect or deepen
- Students who quote and reference the text directly in their dialogue, citing chapter numbers and/or page references if convenient
- At least one genuine interpretive disagreement
- Moments of productive silence or uncertainty
- A student changing their mind or refining their position
- Natural arc: entry/orientation, exploration of competing readings, complication/tension, and movement toward synthesis
- Stage directions in brackets for key moments: [pause], [flipping pages], [nodding], [turning to address another student], etc.
- Real high school voices: smart, engaged, articulate, but still teenagers — not professors

OPENING QUESTION FOR THE TEACHER
The teacher should begin with exactly one rich question about whether Gatsby’s dream is exposed as pure illusion, a genuine form of longing, or both, and how Fitzgerald uses the green light and Nick’s narration to make that ambiguity matter.

STYLE AND CONSTRAINTS
- The dialogue should be text-anchored throughout
- Do not turn this into a literary analysis essay
- Do not include headings, bullet points, or explanatory commentary
- Do not have the teacher lecture
- Make the discussion feel alive, messy, and authentic
- End with the teacher’s brief synthesis move that names one or two insights the group has discovered without fully resolving them

Output only the script itself.

harkness_discussion_prompts.title: The Things They Carried — Burden, Story, and Truth
harkness_discussion_prompts.prompt:
You are to write a realistic Harkness discussion DIALOGUE SCRIPT for EN421: The Novel.

COURSE CONTEXT
- Code: EN421
- Title: The Novel
- Description: Devoted to the study of long-form fiction. Reading materials include novels by diverse authors from the nineteenth and twentieth centuries. Analytical writing plays a central role with students composing papers on each of three novels. Discussion-based inquiry around the Harkness table.
- Tags: novel, long-form fiction, analytical writing, Harkness, 19th century, 20th century

SCHOOL CONTEXT
- Lawrenceville School, boarding school, grades 9-12
- Students are strong readers and capable of discussion at an undergraduate seminar level
- The classroom uses the Harkness method: a student-led discussion around a table

THE HARKNESS DISCUSSION METHOD
Harkness discussion is a student-led, collaborative inquiry method developed at Phillips Exeter Academy. It is the cornerstone pedagogy at the Lawrenceville School and other leading independent schools.

SETUP: Students sit in an oval or rectangle (the "Harkness table") so every participant can see every other participant. The teacher sits at the table as an equal presence but does NOT moderate or call on students.

- Reference the text directly (open books on the table, cite passages)

- Ask follow-up questions of each other (not the teacher)
- Tolerate productive silence — not every pause needs filling
- Disagree respectfully and with textual evidence

- Opens with a single, rich question and then steps back
- Maps the discussion (tracks who speaks, who builds on whom)
- Intervenes ONLY to redirect a stalled discussion, push deeper, or surface a neglected thread — never to provide "the answer"
- Closes the discussion with a synthesis move that helps students name what they collectively discovered

- It is TEXT-ANCHORED: students must have the text open to answer it
- It is GENUINELY CONTESTED: reasonable readers can disagree

- It is ACCESSIBLE: every student can enter the conversation, but no student can exhaust it quickly
- It avoids yes/no framing and avoids questions with obvious "right" answers

1. ENTRY — students orient to the question, offer initial readings
2. EXPLORATION — competing interpretations surface, evidence is marshalled
3. COMPLICATION — students discover tensions, contradictions, or layers
4. SYNTHESIS — the group begins to articulate a collective (not unanimous) insight

TEXT FOR THIS DISCUSSION
Novel: The Things They Carried by Tim O’Brien
Focus: "The Things They Carried," "Love," "How to Tell a True War Story," and "The Man I Killed," with emphasis on the relationship between physical burdens, emotional burdens, and storytelling.

Use these verbatim passages in the students’ dialogue. Students should cite chapter/story titles and quote exactly where possible.

1) Opening inventory, "The Things They Carried":
“They carried all they could bear, and then some, including a silent awe for the terrible power of the things they carried.”

2) On the emotional burden, "The Things They Carried":
“Cowardice, for example, or love, though the exact weights were unquantifiable.”

3) On storytelling and truth, "How to Tell a True War Story":
“A thing may happen and be a total lie; another thing may not happen and be truer than the truth.”

4) On war stories, "How to Tell a True War Story":
“A true war story is never moral.”

5) Rat Kiley’s pain, "The Things They Carried":
“Sometimes, I can’t feel my legs.”

6) On memory and guilt, "The Man I Killed":
“He was a slim, dead, almost dainty young man.”

7) On repeating the scene, "The Man I Killed":
“I will tell the story exactly as it happened.”

8) On love and the war, "Love":
“After all this time, maybe I’m still in love.”

9) A key line for uncertainty, "How to Tell a True War Story":
“You can tell a true war story by the way it never seems to end.”

YOUR TASK
Write an ACTUAL dialogue script, not an essay, not notes, or a summary.

The script must feel like a realistic 20-30 minute Harkness table discussion written as a transcript/play script with:
- 6-8 named students using first names only, e.g. Maya, James, Sofia, Ethan, Aaliyah, Noah, Priya
- Distinct voices and perspectives
- The TEACHER who opens with one rich question, then steps back and only intervenes 2-3 times to redirect or deepen
- Students who quote and reference the text directly in their dialogue, citing chapter numbers and/or page references if convenient
- At least one genuine interpretive disagreement
- Moments of productive silence or uncertainty
- A student changing their mind or refining their position
- Natural arc: entry/orientation, exploration of competing readings, complication/tension, and movement toward synthesis
- Stage directions in brackets for key moments: [pause], [flipping pages], [nodding], [turning to address another student], etc.
- Real high school voices: smart, engaged, articulate, but still teenagers — not professors

OPENING QUESTION FOR THE TEACHER
The teacher should begin with exactly one rich question about whether O’Brien makes war story-telling an act of truth, distortion, survival, or self-protection — and how the repeated attention to carried objects and repeated scenes changes what counts as "real" in the book.

STYLE AND CONSTRAINTS
- The dialogue should be text-anchored throughout
- Do not turn this into a literary analysis essay
- Do not include headings, bullet points, or explanatory commentary
- Do not have the teacher lecture
- Make the discussion feel alive, messy, and authentic
- End with the teacher’s brief synthesis move that names one or two insights the group has discovered without fully resolving them

Output only the script itself.

essay_prompts.title: Beloved: Memory, Motherhood, and the Ethics of Remembrance
essay_prompts.prompt: You are writing for EN421, The Novel, a course devoted to long-form fiction, analytical writing, and discussion-based inquiry in the spirit of the Harkness table; write a formal academic argumentative essay of 600–1000 words on Toni Morrison’s Beloved that argues this exact thesis: the novel insists that traumatic memory cannot be healed by repression, but it also shows that remembrance is ethically dangerous when it traps the survivor in the past, so Morrison presents communal witness—not private forgetting and not solitary confession—as the only sustainable way to live with historical pain. Your essay must engage closely with at least three exact passages and quote them verbatim: first, Sethe’s explanation in Chapter 16, “It was not a story to pass on,” because this phrase captures the novel’s paradoxical demand to remember and resist repetition; second, the rememory passage in Chapter 19, where Sethe says, “Someday you be walking down the road and hear something or see something going on. So clear. And if you go there—you never forget it. Even if it is not your own memory,” because this passage defines memory as social and spatial rather than merely private; and third, the concluding scene in Chapter 27, where the narration says, “This is not a story to pass on,” because the ending repeats the earlier line in a way that forces the reader to confront the tension between burial and preservation. You must explicitly rebut the opposing interpretation that the novel simply endorses a therapeutic recovery model in which telling the story straightforwardly cures trauma; instead, argue that Morrison shows healing as collective, incomplete, and morally serious, requiring the presence of the community, the acknowledgment of slavery’s historical violence, and the refusal to let memory become either fetish or silence. Structure the essay in five paragraphs: an introduction with a precise thesis, three body paragraphs devoted respectively to the three required passages, and a conclusion that explains how the novel’s ending transforms remembrance into ethical responsibility. Write in a polished, formal academic register with careful attention to symbolism, repetition, syntax, and the novel’s shifting narrative voices.

lesson_plan_prompts.title: Lesson: Jane Austen, Pride and Prejudice, Chapter 34 — Darcy's First Letter
lesson_plan_prompts.prompt:
You are an experienced English teacher designing a single 45-60 minute class session plan for EN421: The Novel.

SCHOOL CONTEXT: Lawrenceville School, a boarding school serving grades 9-12. Students are capable readers and the class operates at an undergraduate seminar level. Discussion is Harkness-style and analytical writing is central.

COURSE INFORMATION:
Code: EN421
Title: The Novel
Description: Devoted to the study of long-form fiction. Reading materials include novels by diverse authors from the nineteenth and twentieth centuries. Analytical writing plays a central role with students composing papers on each of three novels. Discussion-based inquiry around the Harkness table.
Tags: novel, long-form fiction, analytical writing, Harkness, 19th century, 20th century

SESSION FOCUS: Jane Austen, Pride and Prejudice, Chapter 34 (the scene in which Elizabeth reads Darcy's first letter), with close attention to pages 168-173 in many editions. This should be treated as one specific class meeting on a single narrow topic: how the letter changes Elizabeth's interpretation of Darcy and Wickham.

RELEVANT PASSAGES (use these verbatim in your lesson plan):
1) "The first shock of surprise had scarcely passed away, before she felt that there must be some mistake in the present communication."
2) "You could not have made me the offer of your hand in any possible way that would have tempted me to accept it."
3) "I can easily believe that there may have been little sincerity in your account of what really passed between Mr. Wickham and myself."
4) "I have not the smallest fear that this event will be a disadvantage to me."
5) "Had not my own feelings decided against you — had not your character been so unambiguously stated by your own conduct to be incapable of every thing but self-importance and caprice..."

IMPORTANT: Assume the exact edition may vary, so refer to Chapter 34 and the scene of the letter rather than page-specific details in the plan unless you choose to mention a page range as approximate. The lesson should center on Austen's use of free indirect discourse, self-correction, and shifting judgment.

TASK: Produce a complete, ready-to-use lesson plan for this one class session. The lesson plan must be practical and specific, appropriate for a 45-60 minute seminar session.

REQUIRED OUTPUT SECTIONS:
A) SESSION FOCUS — the exact narrow topic with references
B) LEARNING OBJECTIVES — 3-4 observable outcomes
C) ENTRY ACTIVITY — 5-8 minutes, specific warm-up tied to the focus
D) DIRECT INSTRUCTION / MINI-LESSON — 10 minutes, what the teacher teaches, referencing the specific passage or technique
E) CLOSE READING / DISCUSSION ACTIVITY — 20-25 minutes, central activity with exact passages, method (Harkness, Socratic, pair-share, etc.), and 2-3 lens questions
F) ASSESSMENT / EXIT ACTIVITY — 5-10 minutes, exit ticket, written response, etc.
G) DIFFERENTIATION NOTES — one scaffolding suggestion, one extension suggestion
H) MATERIALS LIST — texts, handouts, multimedia needed

STYLE REQUIREMENTS:
- Make the plan coherent for a single 45-60 minute class.
- Use the provided quotes exactly and integrate them into the plan.
- Keep the focus narrow and analytical; do not turn this into a full-unit plan.
- Write for a skilled literature teacher at an elite secondary school.

lesson_plan_prompts.prompt:
You are an experienced English teacher designing a single 45-60 minute class session plan for EN421: The Novel.

SCHOOL CONTEXT: Lawrenceville School, a boarding school serving grades 9-12. Students are capable readers and the class operates at an undergraduate seminar level. Discussion is Harkness-style and analytical writing is central.

Code: EN421
Title: The Novel
Description: Devoted to the study of long-form fiction. Reading materials include novels by diverse authors from the nineteenth and twentieth centuries. Analytical writing plays a central role with students composing papers on each of three novels. Discussion-based inquiry around the Harkness table.
Tags: novel, long-form fiction, analytical writing, Harkness, 19th century, 20th century

SESSION FOCUS: Mary Shelley, Frankenstein, Volume I, Chapter 5 (the moment of animation and Victor's dream), ideally on pp. 34-36 in many editions. This should be one specific class session on the narrow topic of how Shelley links scientific ambition, bodily horror, and psychological recoil.

RELEVANT PASSAGES (use these verbatim in your lesson plan):
1) "It was on a dreary night of November that I beheld the accomplishment of my toils."
2) "It breathed hard, and a convulsive motion agitated its limbs."
3) "His yellow skin scarcely covered the work of muscles and arteries beneath; his hair was of a lustrous black, and flowing; his teeth of a pearly whiteness..."
4) "the beauty of the dream vanished, and breathless horror and disgust filled my heart."
5) "Unable to endure the aspect of the being I had created, I rushed out of the room..."

IMPORTANT: Assume the exact edition may vary, so refer to Volume I Chapter 5 and the creation scene rather than page-specific details in the plan unless you choose to mention a page range as approximate. The lesson should foreground narration, gothic imagery, and the collapse of Victor's self-conception.

lesson_plan_prompts.title: Lesson: Charles Dickens, Great Expectations, Chapter 8 — Miss Havisham and the Satis House Discovery
lesson_plan_prompts.prompt:
You are an experienced English teacher designing a single 45-60 minute class session plan for EN421: The Novel.

SCHOOL CONTEXT: Lawrenceville School, a boarding school serving grades 9-12. Students are capable readers and the class operates at an undergraduate seminar level. Discussion is Harkness-style and analytical writing is central.

COURSE INFORMATION:
Code: EN421
Title: The Novel
Description: Devoted to the study of long-form fiction. Reading materials include novels by diverse authors from the nineteenth and twentieth centuries. Analytical writing plays a central role with students composing papers on each of three novels. Discussion-based inquiry around the Harkness table.
Tags: novel, long-form fiction, analytical writing, Harkness, 19th century, 20th century

SESSION FOCUS: Charles Dickens, Great Expectations, Chapter 8, especially the first visit to Satis House and Pip's encounter with Miss Havisham. Use the sequence usually found on pp. 57-65 in many editions. This should be one narrow session on class, decay, performance, and Pip's social self-consciousness.

RELEVANT PASSAGES (use these verbatim in your lesson plan):
1) "She was dressed in rich materials — satins, and lace, and silks — all of white."
2) "I saw that everything within my view which ought to be white, had been white long ago, and had lost its lustre..."
3) "She had stopped the clocks at twenty minutes to nine."
4) "The bride within the bridal dress had withered like the dress..."
5) "Take the Haversham."
6) "I am what you call a common laboring-boy."

IMPORTANT: Assume the exact edition may vary, so refer to Chapter 8 and the Satis House scene rather than page-specific details in the plan unless you choose to mention a page range as approximate. The lesson should emphasize Dickens's gothic staging, first-person retrospective narration, and the manipulation of Pip's identity.

lesson_plan_prompts.prompt:
You are an experienced English teacher designing a single 45-60 minute class session plan for EN421: The Novel.

SCHOOL CONTEXT: Lawrenceville School, a boarding school serving grades 9-12. Students are capable readers and the class operates at an undergraduate seminar level. Discussion is Harkness-style and analytical writing is central.

Analytical writing plays a central role with students composing papers on each of three novels. Discussion-based inquiry around the Harkness table.
Tags: novel, long-form fiction, analytical writing, Harkness, 19th century, 20th century

SESSION FOCUS: Toni Morrison, Beloved, Chapter 19, especially Beloved's monologue about crossing water and the fragmented origin story. Use the passage commonly appearing around pp. 62-69 in many editions. This should be one specific class meeting focused on narrative fragmentation, trauma, and memory.

RELEVANT PASSAGES (use these verbatim in your lesson plan):
1) "I am Beloved and she is mine."
2) "I come out of the water."
3) "a hot thing"
4) "small and staticky"
5) "I am looking for the join."
6) "the pieces I am, she gathered them and gave them back to me in all the right order."

IMPORTANT: Assume the exact edition may vary, so refer to Chapter 19 and Beloved's monologue rather than page-specific details in the plan unless you choose to mention a page range as approximate. The lesson should center on Morrison's syntax, repetition, and the ethics of representing the unspeakable.

lesson_plan_prompts.title: Lesson: F. Scott Fitzgerald, The Great Gatsby, Chapter 7 — Plaza Hotel Confrontation
lesson_plan_prompts.prompt:
You are an experienced English teacher designing a single 45-60 minute class session plan for EN421: The Novel.

SCHOOL CONTEXT: Lawrenceville School, a boarding school serving grades 9-12. Students are capable readers and the class operates at an undergraduate seminar level. Discussion is Harkness-style and analytical writing is central.

Title: The Novel
Description: Devoted to the study of long-form fiction. Reading materials include novels by diverse authors from the nineteenth and twentieth centuries. Analytical writing plays a central role with students composing papers on each of three novels. Discussion-based inquiry around the Harkness table.
Tags: novel, long-form fiction, analytical writing, Harkness, 19th century, 20th century

SESSION FOCUS: F. Scott Fitzgerald, The Great Gatsby, Chapter 7, the Plaza Hotel confrontation and the collapse of the Buchanan/Gatsby/Daisy arrangement. Use the scene usually found on pp. 128-136 in many editions. This should be one narrow class session on rhetoric, emotional exposure, and the limits of reinvention.

RELEVANT PASSAGES (use these verbatim in your lesson plan):
1) "Her voice is full of money."
2) "Jay Gatsby, of West Egg, Long Island, sprang from his Platonic conception of himself."
3) "I did love him once — but I loved you too."
4) "You're acting like a little boy."
5) "Her voice had the misleading, thrilling quality of a sentence in quotation marks."
6) "There are only the pursued, the pursuing, the busy and the tired."

IMPORTANT: Assume the exact edition may vary, so refer to Chapter 7 and the Plaza Hotel scene rather than page-specific details in the plan unless you choose to mention a page range as approximate. The lesson should foreground dialogue, symbolism, social performance, and Nick's retrospective narration.
